serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Thai steamed egg with prawn and minced chicken is approximately 300 g, served as a single bowl or plate at a Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed egg with minced pork and prawn — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central entries for whole egg (FDC ID 748967), cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), and ground chicken (FDC ID 171477), weighted to reflect a typical Thai steamed egg recipe (approximately 55% egg, 20% prawn, 15% minced chicken, 10% broth/seasoning).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai steamed egg with prawn and minced chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central entries for whole egg, cooked shrimp, and ground chicken, adjusted for water dilution from steaming broth. Energy calculated from Atwater factors. Sodium elevated to reflect fish sauce seasoning typical of Thai preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg-based and protein-dominant mixed dishes — analogous estimate based on steamed egg (whole egg, low-carbohydrate preparation) reference data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai steamed egg with prawn and minced chicken. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database reference for whole egg preparations and protein-dominant mixed dishes. The dish is very low in carbohydrate (~1.8 g per 100 g), so the glycemic load per 300 g serving is approximately 1.6, which is negligible. Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care confirms egg-based dishes are consistently low-GI. GL = (30 × 5.4 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 1.6.

hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, which represents more than half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or consume smaller portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only approximately 5.4 g of carbohydrate per 300 g serving and a glycemic index of 30, this dish produces a negligible glycemic load of approximately 1.6 and is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though sodium content should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol from egg and sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving contains approximately 585 mg dietary cholesterol from egg yolks and approximately 1260 mg sodium from fish sauce; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and discuss egg consumption frequency with their clinician, as current evidence on dietary cholesterol and CVD risk is nuanced.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai steamed egg with prawn and minced chicken (ไข่ตุ๋นกุ้งและไก่บด, romanized: Khai Tun Kung lae Kai Bot) is a classic Thai comfort dish consisting of silky, custard-like steamed whole eggs combined with fresh prawns and finely minced chicken, seasoned with fish sauce and sometimes a small amount of oyster sauce or white pepper. The dish is prepared by whisking eggs with broth or water to achieve a smooth, airy batter, then adding raw prawn and minced chicken before steaming gently over low heat until the egg sets into a soft, wobbly, pudding-like texture. The result is a delicate, savoury custard with tender pieces of prawn and chicken embedded throughout. The flavour profile is mild, umami-rich, and slightly salty from the fish sauce, with a smooth, silky mouthfeel that contrasts with the firmer prawn and chicken pieces. In Thailand, this dish is commonly served as part of a shared rice-based meal and is considered a mild, easily digestible option suitable for all ages. Nutritionally, it is high in protein (approximately 10.2 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (5.4 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrate (1.8 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (GI approximately 30) with a negligible glycemic load per serving. It is rich in vitamin B12, vitamin A, selenium, and phosphorus from the egg and seafood components. The primary clinical concern is sodium content from fish sauce, which may be relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai steamed egg with prawn and minced chicken was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for whole egg, cooked shrimp, and ground chicken. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for egg-based low-carbohydrate dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component ingredients are well-characterised in USDA FoodData Central and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (whole egg FDC 748967, cooked shrimp FDC 175180, ground chicken FDC 171477) weighted at approximately 55% egg, 20% prawn, 15% minced chicken, 10% broth/seasoning. 2. Sodium is elevated to 420 mg per 100 g to reflect fish sauce seasoning typical of Thai restaurant preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — egg, prawn, and chicken contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant sources are absent in these ingredients). 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this dish contains no plant-based fibre sources). 5. GI estimated at 30 (low) as an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for egg-based preparations; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 5.4) / 100 = 1.6. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are approximate. 7. The food_name has been normalised from the menu label '420D Steamed Egg with Prawn and Minced C' (where 'C' is interpreted as 'Chicken' based on the Sanook Kitchen menu context) to the canonical English name 'Steamed Egg with Prawn and Minced Chicken'. 8. This dish is classified under food_group 'Fish and seafood' as per the input record, which is defensible given the prawn component, though 'Dairy and eggs' or 'Mixed' could also apply. 9. Halal status is assumed true as the dish contains no pork or alcohol; however, fish sauce brand and sourcing should be verified for formal halal certification.
